title: Threat indicators in SecOps
question: What are threat indicators and how would you explain them in a SecOps interview?
summary: Explains why indicators must be normalized, scored, and tied to investigations carefully.
exactAnswer: Threat indicators are observables such as hashes, IP addresses, URLs, or domains that represent potentially malicious activity. In interviews, explain that raw indicators are only useful when they are normalized, sourced, scored, de-duplicated, and connected to cases or detections in a governed way.
keyPoints: Indicators are observables, not conclusions on their own.
Indicators should enrich detections and investigations.
examples: Inbound feed of malicious domains normalized before analyst review.

title: Security case handoff basics
question: How do you explain case handoff in SecOps?
summary: Explains routing detections into repeatable analyst workflows.
exactAnswer: Security case handoff is the process of routing alerts, enriched indicators, or correlation results into a case or incident workflow that analysts can triage and escalate consistently. The interview-ready point is that detection without operational handoff creates noise, while governed handoff creates measurable response work.
keyPoints: Routing and triage ownership are critical.
Case transitions should align to analyst workflow stages.
examples: High-confidence alert opens a security incident with enrichment attached.

title: Vulnerability grouping basics
question: Why do vulnerability groups matter in SecOps?
summary: Explains why grouping keeps remediation work actionable.
exactAnswer: Vulnerability groups consolidate related vulnerable items into a manageable remediation work unit. In interviews, explain that grouping helps teams assign ownership, reduce ticket noise, and prioritize remediation around a system, owner, or campaign instead of treating every finding in isolation.
keyPoints: Grouping reduces alert fatigue and work sprawl.
Groups support assignment and coordinated remediation.
Grouping logic should align to ownership and remediation strategy.
examples: Group all critical server findings for one application team into one remediation stream.

title: SecOps prioritization basics
question: How do you explain prioritization in SecOps implementations?
summary: Shows how severity, confidence, business context, and exposure combine in triage.
exactAnswer: A good SecOps implementation prioritizes not only by technical severity, but also by confidence, exploitability, business criticality, asset exposure, and whether there is meaningful threat context attached.
keyPoints: Priority should combine technical and business context.
Consistent prioritization is essential for analyst trust.
examples: Critical vulnerability on a public-facing payment server outranks a lab asset finding.
